Now, all that said... there are times when this time-continuum headline does NOT
work. For example... on the pop-ups on my site, I noticed the time-continuum headline
getting beat or just breaking even with my other (non time-continuum) pop-up headline.
This threw me for a loop (he had not tested pop-ups yet with this, to my knowledge I'm
the first to have done so). So I askcdjim and here's what he said:
if i 	had to guess, 	it's the frame of mind they're coming in. when
someone reaches your squeeze page, 	it's because they're going there to
get something specific. 	time continuum gives them sense of urgency to
move forward and reinforces the action they have come there for.
on the other hand, 	the popup version is made for the average site
visitor. 	it depends on "interruption". 	and the generality of the
message is probably what's making it outpull.
it would be interesting to try a time continuum with a general message.
basically, what i 	think has happened is we wrote the time continuum FOR
a squeeze page, 	so it does well there. whereas we never really thought
about it as a popup.
So there you have it. This is very advanced info and you may have to read through
it a few times to really get the hang of it. But once you do, don't be shocked if you sec
your opt-ins (and thus, SALES, especially if you email daily) increase. You never know
until you test it, so give it a whirl and see what happens ...
How To ''MisQuote'' Your Way
To More Profits With Etnail
Okay, now let's move on to something else. One of the coolest emails you can use
(that your competition is probably not doing, which means you will stick out like a sore
thumb if YOU use it) is the "misquotation email." This is exactly like what it sounds like:
You build your entire email around a misquote - where you replace a few words with
something relevant to what YOU sell-- from someone else (maybe "paraphrase" is more
accurate than misquote ... ). It can be anyone you want, too. A celebrity, religious leader,
business author, it simply does not matter. It makes it fun for your readers and all that
wisdom you quote kind of "oozes" off onto you so it's almost like you're being endorsed
by whoever you're quoting. Here is an example of what it could look like:

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
I did not invent this by any means. Like most people, I spent YEARS doing hard
teaching on my blog and in my emails and got the same lackluster results most people
who do such activities get (little or no sales, and a horde of pissant freebie seekers
descending on my site and then turning on me like a rabid dog when I -- GASP! -- tried
to sell something). Then, one day, I bought Matt Furey's old email course (NOT the
AWAI one, I have no idea how to get his older course anymore, so do not ask ...) and a
whole new world was opened up to me. He was very insistent in that course on rarely (or
never) hard teaching -- and instead finding ways to sell, but in a way that looks, feels and
smells like teaching. Do it right and, yes, in a way, you ARE teaching. But, you're just not
teaching what's in your paid course, or anything they can use without actually buying
your wares (which you will be so generous as to provide a link to). That way, in order to
execute what you are writing about, they will need to pony up the dough or be left
wandering around in the wilderness trying to do what you teach, but never succeeding at
it or struggling with it so much they eventually do come back and buy from you. (This is
not a "trick" either-- you're doing what's in their best interest~ everyone knows--
assuming they are intellectually honest -- that nobody puts as much value on free as they
do on things they pay for. That means, by selling them your product~ there is a MUCH
greater chance of them using it and fixing whatever problem they bought it for in the first
place ... )
OK, enough build up.
Time for some examples! I will cover this more in future issues. And, if you're
paying attention, you'll notice I do this in almost every single email I send out (so mostly
all of the examples in each of these issues is an illustration of how to do it, as well as if
you're on my daily email list at BenSettle.com).
Anyway, here's a real life recent example of what I'm talking about. And yes, it did
extremely well. But before you read it, note how I don't hard teach, but I DO give
information. The reader cannot implement what is in the paid product via this email, but
they've been given enough information to make it worth reading, and that will actually
HELP them get the benefit they want when they finally do buy the product.

tell great stories, air out their problems, and let you see what emotions your market is
feeling.

subject Line: Why even "health nuts" have prostate problems
Hi,
Sometimes doctors are like feuding families.
You got the mainstream ones touting drugs and surgery as the grand
answer to life's medical woes ••• 	and then you have the natural
doctors who say all prescription drugs are the devil.
I've been to both kinds.
And, 	I 	usually fall on the natural side of things.
(Which makes me the black sheep in my family -- my mom is a nurse
working for a pro-drug doctor, 	and my brother is a pharmaceutical
drug rep ••• )
But, 	you know what?
There is one thing they agree on:
The devastating effects of inflammation.
Inflammation is terrible for your body.
It makes you more prone to injury, 	and more likely to get sick,
gain unsightly body fat, 	have sexual dysfunction, 	catch a disease
and have overall terrible health.
And you know what the worst part is?
Most people are in a state of inflammation.
Yes, 	even so-called "health nuts."
This is especially true if you live in an industrialized nation --
with all the pollution, 	tainted food, 	refined sugar (which is added
to everything, 	even salt!), 	stress and the list goes on.
So inflammation sucks.
It's also REALLY bad for your prostate.
Hey, you got prostate pain?

Again, not much hard instruction. Yes, they can go online now and look up ways to
get rid of inflammation, etc. But most people are busy, want the expert to give them the
solution (that they trust) so they can save time (aren't we all like that?). It IS informational
and educational ... but it's not hard instruction.
That's why I call this "soft" teaching (as opposed to hard teaching).
As you can see, soft teaching is still teaching -- but it's more informational rather
than instructional. If you look at 95°/o of the emails I send out to BenSettle.com you will
see the same thing. I'm giving information that's interesting to people interested in email
and business, etc. But it's rarely ever hard instruction. Sometimes it's good to do hard
instruction (like if you want to build some high credibility every now and then by showing
people how jeenius you are). But mostly it's best to do soft teaching-- interesting, fun,
educational, but not instructional. (fhe only "instruction" you give is when you instruct
them on where to go to order by providing them a link haha -- c'mon you KNOW that
was funny. .. )
Best part about this? There are MANY ways of doing it.
As I've mentioned (too many times) in upcoming issues I'm going to be giving you
more examples and instruction about this. It's extremely important and profitable, and it
will make you many more dineros.

Ben: We're talking with Jim Yaghi ofwww.JimYaghi.com. Jim by training is a computer scientist. He

Ben: We're going to talk about all kinds of cool stuff, and the first thing I want to ask you about, Jim,
because I've found this to be one of the most interesting ways to boost response that I've seen in years,
and that is this thing that I kind of dubbed "the time continuum headline." What is that and how did you
come up with this? We can give them examples too if we want.
Jim: It doesn't really have anything to do with time travel, but the idea behind it is it's ju t a way of
thinking about the language you use when you create your landing page copy. It could also be about your
ads or pretty much any kind of copywriting when you want somebody to move to a second step, whether
that's a click or sign-up to your mailing Jist or even to order.
The way I like to say it is if you were to sort of draw a line between where they arc right now, and think
of it like in a physical space like a room or something, and you draw a line, this is a barrier here where
they're on one side. Say they're outside the room. On the inside is what you have on the landing page,
basically, so ifthat's a sign-up to your mailing list then you're going to give them something in exchange.
You're going to send them an email with something they're requesting.
You want to put yourself right there, right at that point where they re just about to step through, the first
thing they see when lbey open that door.
How I like to visualize it when I write is it really comes down to using very current language in the words
that you use. It's a very systematic effort to remove anything that's conditional, and we'll talk a little more
about what that means, but to remove conditional statements, to remove anything that is future-based or
past-based in terms of the words that you use, the verbs that you use.
You want to have these active verbs that are about what is going on currently right now in front of you. So
you have these very present-tense verbs, and all the language is about the current. The current is really,
really an important feature in this.

Jim: That's the other thing we've been talking about over this last month or so, the split tests. We're
finding more and more that they're not useful. We have these experiments and they start out and you're
like, "Wow, a drastic difference, so this one's the better-performing ad or the better-performing landing
page because of this ."
It's all hypothetical, all theory. It's ju st a best guess. "We think the reason this did better is because of
this." It sounds really intelligent and it sounds like it's logical and everything because this is how we're
able to justify it, but it's just one possible theory. It could be a completely different reason.
One of the things that I've sa id to you before, Ben, was that the content of split testing is about testing
two things, one variable in two different states. For example, a font color scheme of red or blu e. This is
one variable, the font color, being the color red or blue. Typically when we do split tests we don' t actually
test just one variable being changed into two states. There are a lot of other parameters that go into it.
I don't want to over-complicate this, but statistically split tests; or the concept that split tests are built
upon, basically require evezything to be constant. The problem we have is that people and their reactions
show chaotic behavior or random behavior.
You show your ad, which in the flrst place if you've done two different versions of it, the two versions are
random and you put them in front of a random pool of people, a sample of the population. Then from that
population their reactions are also random because it may be a different time of day, it may be different
based on what's been happening before in their lives, their experience and all that -all very random. Then

Really I would suggest don't kill off one version and favor the other. Keep both. In this particular

Jim: Click tracking tends to be more reliable. Email open rates - mo st people quote these a lot more than
click rates. Op en rates is an estimate really. You can compare one open rate against another in your
mailing list, but I wouldn ' t really put a Lot of stock in it, the main reason be ing- this is a little bit of a
technical explanation of what happens when tracking is done -
Suppose you just go to any web page on the internet, just to side track for a minute from email. Go to any
web page on the internet. What happens is your browser sends a request back to the server that has that
web page available. It says, "Bring me this web page," so it downloads this web page. All it se nds is one
file, which is the HTML text document.
So it grabs this docume nt and then my browser, or your client's browser, will look at that file and go
through and find any other things that need to load, like images, movies, JavaScript files, any of that sort
of thing, and it will take them in tum and request them one by one from the server. So it sends back
another request to the server and asks, "Send me this first, send me this second, send me this third," and
so on. That's really bow any internet-based downloading happens for just about anything, specifically
when we talk about HTML ema il s and web pages.

When an autoresponder like Aweber reports open rates, what they do is they include in the HTML-only
version of any email you send out a transparent image. It's an image tag and it says, "Make a request back
to the Aweber server when you lo ad this email." When the email is sent out to the subscrib er or reader, tJ1e
reader receives the email and opens it in their browser, so it loads an HTML page.
Theoretically what 's going to happen is when it hits that transparent image, which he's not going to see
with his own eyes but the browser will see, it sends back a request to Aweber and says, "Please load this
image for. me." In that image request is a little tracking thing that says 1 "Okay, prospect XYZ has ju st
opened the email," so that's bow they're able to track that that email was delivered and subsequently
opened by this particular subscriber.
...
Here's where the problem lies, though. There's two things that stop this number from being accurate,
because otherwise it would be an accurate measure. There's two things. One of them is that not every
single email client or every ema il user bas enabled HTML emails. If you open up th e email on your
phone, for example, it's very likely that on your phone it doesn't ~og that email was opened because it's
not HTML-based, it's text-based, which means there's only one file, the text file that was sect to you and
that's it. It never sent back to the server a request for an image.
Ben: That's interesting because, let's face it, more and more people are reading their cma il s first and
foremost on their phones. I hadn't even thought about it until now.
Jim: If the email client is on the phone, there's a very good chance they're not loading HTML-based
email, so no request is made and no tracking is possible. That's a whole bunch of people that may have
opened your email, or maybe they didn't, that you have never been able to track that they opened it.
There's another problem, and this is much more common. Suppose you use Gmail or Hotmail or any of
these web-based emai l applications, and some of the desktop ones like Outlook as well. It would be the
same thing, and they do this for your protection as an email user. What they do is they suppress images
unless you manually say, "Please load images from here" or you tell them, "This sender is safe, always
download images from them.''
If you look at the very top ofGmail I know that this happens. At the very top it says, " Display images in
this email." Tt's a Link, you can click it, and that will load tbe email message for you and load it with
im ages in the account. If there is no image in the ema il you're not really missing out on anything. Now
we're talking about subscribers. The subscriber doesn't feel like they're missing out on anything, so
they're not going to load images. They're not going to request images in the first place.
Again, even though you loaded an HTML-based email in your browser, they' re not se nding a request
back to the server - the Aweber server or the iContact server or whatever it is you use for your email
sending- to say ''Download the tracking image." So again they 're not being tracked.
-
Why do email brow se rs do this? Gmail does this to protect their users from spam. What a lot of spammers
do is they compile a really long list of emails- stolen emails, they scrape the web and compile all these
different emails, there's all sorts of different techniques they use, I don't know because I'm not a
spammer - but I know that some of their techniques are like this.
They pull up a giant list of emails and then they send a message to them. They send the message but they
don'tknow that it's being read or it's being opened or the email address even exists or that it 's valid, so
what they do is they try a ll these kinds of these sneaky tricks to get you to say click a link.
Sometimes they say, "To remove yourselffrom the list, click this Link.'' Don't click that link, because if
you click it there's a good chance it 's going to actually subscribe you into their s ub - li st whkh stores all
these list of the cmails that are valid, tbe real ones. It doesn't mean just because you sec an unsubscribe
lick on there you should click it. Just make sure it's from a proper place like Aweber, for example.

Jim: A lot of people confuse my sales letters with the Ryan Deiss-style sales letter. Certainly there are
similarities. I'm not going to deny the similarities here, and I may have been inspired by those in the first
place.
The main difference between the Ryan Deiss-style video sales letter, which a Jot of people have adopted,
and the one that 1 do is his sales letters tend to have long blocks of text. He grabs paragraph by paragraph
and puts them on a page and then be basically tells the sales letter.
The main reason that I had to come up with this, and this is what inspired the idea, was that I found that T
have a lot of hesitation when I do recordings of video and audio and I ended up spending a lot of time
editing. [n the first place, when I was trying to get quality recordings out, especially for sales, it's not cool
to have a lot of hesitation in a sales video. It can be very irritating to keep hearing it, and when you're
editing it, it's even more irritating.
What I did was I would begin by writing a presentation in writing, and then I would go and I would create
a PowerPoint with the different slides and visuals and all that stuff to go along with it, and plan out
basically the production aspect, then I'd record it. That's the third phase. I'd record it, usually say 10
slides at a time or something like that, and read off my transcript.
I tried different ways to do it. I tried putting it on another computer. I tried printing it out and editing it on
paper and things like that, marking where I want to pause and stuff like that. It was really a complicated
thing, and once that was done I'd still have to go back and edit everything together and make it
presentable. A 20-minutc video could take me like three days to produce, and this was at my fastest rate.
In order to solve that problem, what I did was I started to create slides of what J wanted to say, and I
would just read the slides and just scroll through them as I recorded the presentation. This helped me to
decide all that stuff like where to pause, the intonation and things like that, and I could always go back ifl
made a mistake. I'd keep rolling and just go back and continue from wherever that was, and it was easy
enough to edit.
The main reason this is a successful method is because of the way that I break up tlle text. I use very short
bursts rather than big paragraph blocks. I use extremely short bursts - one, two, sometimes three lines in
large font- and that's really the biggest point of distinction between the videos sales letters done by Deiss
and a lot of these other people who then imitate it.
The cool thing about it is it's getting the person to read along with you. Rather, you're reading along with
them. You're aiding them in their reading. It's like sub-titles. They're reading and you're announcing for
them. Some people are slower readers. Some are not that fluent. Sometimes they don't know how
pronounce the words or something like that. With you pronouncing it for them, you doing the intonation
for them, you're helping them, you're aiding them in the reading. As well as that, because you're focused
on a very small amount of text, you're forcing them to interactively engage with your sales message and
keep their eyes locked.
Now here's the thing that's really important, because I've seen these video sales letters extend for like an
hour long. Those would make extremely crappy video sales letters in my opinion, because it's extremely
straining to make somebody focus for that long. You're forcing them to focus for an entire hour? I've
clicked out of a lot of these long video sales letters within the first few 10-15 minutes.

Jim: Apart from presentation, just being more professional and all that, the music I use is always a loop,

exact words to use here, but if you notice when they do hypnosis what do they do? They bang a pendulum
and they move it back and forth in front of your eyes and you follow it.
At first it's kind of hard to focus. You have to actually use your conscious mind to tell your eyes to move
from side to side, and they do this with audio as well. I have done hypnosis through audio, and it's always
done through loops.
It' s the same pattern, just repeated over and over, and then eventually because you're into the pattern now,
your brain is into the pattern of just repeating this same thing over and over, that just goes into the
subconscious. Then everything else that you want to consciously program in goes on top and in another
layer. With that background loop, initially you notice, and then afterwards it just becomes part of the
background.
